title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
In Step 3, you learned about serverless Shiny Apps with “Shinylive.” What is Shinylive? Assume that you are also explaining it to your coworkers. How does it differ from the traditional Shiny app introduced in Step 2? What are the pros and cons of Shinylive in comparison with Shiny Apps? These lecture notes will become your “Essay on Shiny Live” part of the website.

Shinylive is a deployment method that compiles your standard Shiny app into a static set of web files. It accomplishes this using a technology called WebAssembly (Wasm) via an underlying tool called WebR. Essentially, WebAssembly allows a web browser to run complex software directly on a user’s computer. When someone visits a Shinylive URL, their browser temporarily downloads a fully functional, lightweight version of R right into their tab. The difference from the traditional Shiny is where the math happens. One pro of Shinylive is that the app compiles into static HTML, JS, and CSS files. You don’t need an expensive R server. You can host your apps completely free on static platforms like GitHub Pages. Another pro is traditional servers crash if an app “goes viral” because the server gets overloaded processing requests. With Shinylive, 10,000 people can use the app at the exact same time without a stutter, because every user provides their own computational power. A third pro is once published to a static host, the app will essentially live forever without maintenance or ongoing server bills. This is perfect for accompanying academic papers or public company portfolios. One con of Shinylive is that the entire R environment and application script are downloaded directly into the user’s browser and anyone can peek into the source code. You cannot use hidden API keys, database passwords, or proprietary proprietary data models. Another con is the first time a user visits a Shinylive app, there is a 5 to 10-second delay while the browser downloads the WebR bundle and required R packages. A third co is not every R package works in a WebAssembly environment yet.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
In Step 2, you learned how to build an interactive web app using Shiny. Let’s assume that your coworkers are interested in learning about it and assume that you are helping them. Create a lecture note that you can use to teach the person. This will become part of the website under “Essay on Shiny Apps.”

The biggest hurdle people face when sharing data analysis is that static charts don’t let stakeholders ask their own questions. Shiny completely solves this. It is an R package that transforms your scripts into interactive web applications without requiring you to learn HTML, CSS, or JavaScript. Every Shiny app follows a strict, predictable structure. It is powered by three main components combined in a single file named app.R.

The User Interface (UI): This controls the layout and appearance of your webpage. It’s where you define what users see (inputs like dropdowns or sliders, and outputs like charts or tables).
The Server Function: This acts as the brain of your app. It holds the logic and standard R code needed to compute data, generate plots, and respond to user inputs.
The Activation Call: The shinyApp(ui, server) function which ties the two pieces together and boots up the app.

To make an app interactive, you have to pass data from the UI to the Server, let the Server process it, and send it back to the UI. Think of it like a conversation:
In your ui, you must explicitly define Inputs (for users to interact with) and Outputs (empty frames waiting for content). Every input and output must have a unique, quoted ID name. Inputs always end in Input(). Outputs always end in Output(). Inside the server function, you write the calculations. To send a plot or text back to the UI, you must wrap your standard R code inside a specific render function that matches your UI placeholder.
In a normal R script, code runs from top to bottom once. In Shiny, code runs reactively. Shiny constantly listens to the variables inside the input object. The moment a user adjusts a slider or checks a box, every piece of code on the server that relies on that specific input automatically re-runs.

Always separate UI elements with a comma.
Missing a comma is the number one reason a Shiny app throws a critical syntax error upon launch. RStudio will usually flag this with a red circle next to the line number.

To Launch: Open your app.R file in RStudio. You will see a Run App button with a green play icon at the top right of the script editor. Alternatively, use the shortcut Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + Enter. To Stop: While the app is running, your R console is “busy” and locked. To close the app and free up your console, hit the Esc key or click the red Stop sign icon in the top right corner of the R console panel.
